examples.title: Separate Chaining Implementation
examples.description: Demonstrates separate chaining collision resolution technique where each hash table slot contains a linked list to store multiple key-value pairs that hash to the same index.
examples.explanation: 1. We create a Node class to represent each key-value pair in the linked list. 2. HashTableChaining initializes an array of size 'size'. 3. The _hash function computes hash index using modulo operation. 4. Insert adds to the end of linked list (or updates if key exists). 5. Search traverses the linked list at that index. 6. Delete removes the node from the linked list. This approach ensures all elements are stored even with collisions.

examples.title: Linear Probing Implementation
examples.description: Demonstrates linear probing, an open addressing collision resolution technique where collisions are resolved by checking the next available slot in a linear sequence.
examples.explanation: 1. Linear probing uses a simple formula: new_index = (hash(key) + i) % size where i increments on each collision. 2. Insert finds the first empty slot or updates if key exists. 3. Search continues probing until finding the key or hitting an empty slot. 4. Delete marks the slot as None but may cause issues - shown here as simple deletion. 5. Primary clustering is the main drawback where long sequences of filled slots form, degrading performance.

examples.title: Double Hashing Implementation
examples.description: Demonstrates double hashing, an advanced open addressing technique that uses two hash functions to reduce primary and secondary clustering by generating different probe sequences for different keys.
examples.explanation: 1. Double hashing uses two hash functions: h1(key) = hash(key) % size and h2(key) = prime - (hash(key) % prime). 2. The probe formula becomes: (h1(key) + i * h2(key)) % size where i is the probe number. 3. h2 must be non-zero and relatively prime to table size for full cycle. 4. This method eliminates primary clustering (similar to linear probing) and reduces secondary clustering (similar to quadratic probing). 5. Each key gets a unique probe sequence based on h2, providing better distribution and improved average case performance.
